text: 20 Lecture 20: Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT)
20.1 Discrete Fourier Transform

The discrete Fourier transform is defined as a transformation on N complex numbers $y _ { 0 } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N - 1 }$ yielding N complex numbers $z _ { 0 } , z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { N - 1 }$ defined by:
Note the analogy with the usual Fourier transform of a function $f ( x )$ :

where the sum on the discrete index $j$ is replaced by an integral on the continuous “index” $x ,$ and $y _ { j }$ and $z _ { k }$ correspond respectively to $f ( x )$ and $\tilde { f } ( \boldsymbol { k } )$ .
20.2 Quantum Fourier Transform
It is a unitary operation QFT defined on basis vectors $| 0 \rangle , | 1 \rangle , . . . , | N - 1 \rangle$ as

Then the components $y _ { j }$ of a vector $\begin{array} { r } { | v \rangle = \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } y _ { j } | j \rangle } \end{array}$ transform under QFT into new components $z _ { k }$ given by the discrete Fourier transform (prove it). We will show in the following that $Q F T$ is indeed unitary.

Using n qubits, the dimension N of the vector space of physical states is $2 ^ { n }$ , and the basis vectors $| 0 \rangle , . . . , | j \rangle , . . . , | 2 ^ { n } - 1 \rangle$ are given by the $2 ^ { n }$ vectors of the computational basis $| 0 0 \cdots 0 \rangle , . . . , | j _ { 1 } j _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot j _ { n } \rangle , . . . , | 1 1 \cdot \cdot \cdot 1 \rangle$ , where $j _ { 1 } , j _ { 2 } , . . . , j _ { n }$ is the binary expression of the integer $j$ .

For example $R _ { 1 } = Z , R _ { 2 } = S , R _ { 3 } = T$ , and in general $R _ { k } ^ { 2 } = R _ { k - 1 }$ .
The above circuit implements the QFT on the n-qubit input state $| j \rangle = | j _ { 1 } \rangle \cdots | j _ { n } \rangle$
Proof: after the first H gate, the input state becomes (prove it):
Applying next the controlled- $R _ { 2 }$ gate produces the state (prove it):

Applying the same procedure to the second qubit leads to:

and repeating it on all the other input qubits yields the final state that coincides with the output of $Q F T | j _ { 1 } j _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot j _ { n } \rangle$ in (20.7), once we invert the order of the output qubits with CROSSOVER gates.

The circuit proves that $Q F T$ is unitary, since all gates are unitary. The circuit uses $n + ( n - 1 ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + 1 = n ( n + 1 ) / 2 = \Theta ( n ^ { 2 } )$ gates. In contrast, the best classical algorithms for the discrete Fourier transform use $\Theta ( n 2 ^ { n } )$ gates, so that $\mathrm { Q F T }$ has an exponential advantage on its classical counterpart.
